children.value: For most of its history, machine learning was used to
things: is this email spam, is this image a cat, does this MRI scan show a tumour? Generative AI flips the question around: instead of

This change is small in mathematics and enormous in practice. It is also the change that took machine learning from a back-office technology into something people use in the foreground, every day. This chapter introduces the vocabulary you will need for the rest of the book.
Suppose we have a dataset of pictures of cats. A
“given an image, output 1 if it is a cat, 0 otherwise.”
“produce an image that looks like the cats you have seen.”
(cat / not-cat). The generator learns about a
(the space of plausible cat pictures). The distribution is much harder to learn — but once you have it, you can sample from it and get pictures that did not exist before.
This is the single mental shift behind everything in this book.
You do not need formal probability for this course, but two ideas help.

each possible thing is. For example, the heights of UiO students form a distribution: 1.70 m is more likely than 2.20 m. A generative image model implicitly learns a distribution over images. A generative language model learns a distribution over sequences of words.
The catch: the space of all possible images is unimaginably large. A single

children.html: 512×512512 \times 512512×512

children.value: colour image has 786 432 numbers in it. The set of
images is a vanishingly thin slice of that vast space. Learning to navigate that slice is what diffusion models, GANs, and transformers do.
is to draw a single concrete thing from a distribution. Every time you press “generate” in an image tool, the model is
from the distribution it learned. Press the button twice and you get two different samples — usually similar in style, never identical.
(text and audio models) — high temperature flattens the distribution, making the output more varied and weird. Low temperature sharpens it, making the output safer and more predictable.
(text models) — restrict the model to the top
% of next-token candidates. Stops it from sampling extremely rare nonsense.
(image and video models) — strengthens the influence of the conditioning prompt. Too high and the result becomes oversaturated and rigid; too low and it ignores the prompt.
(diffusion models) — how many denoising steps to take. More steps generally means sharper output, up to a point.
The same prompt with different sampler settings can produce dramatically different results. This is one of the most useful things to internalise this semester.
Conditioning: telling the model what you want

children.label: Conditioning: telling the model what you want

children.value: If a generator just samples from its full distribution, you get something randomly cat-shaped. Useful, but not creative
. The technical word for steering the generator is
Conditioning is anything that goes into the model
is the most common form of conditioning.
can also condition the generation (“make it look like this”).
can condition where to change (“only edit this part of the picture”).
like a pose skeleton, edge map, or depth map can condition shape and composition (this is what tools like
can condition the next frame in a video model.
Once you start looking, you will see conditioning everywhere. The user interface of every generative tool is essentially a

children.alt: Diagram showing different conditioning inputs (text, image, mask, control) feeding into a single generative model.

children.value: Multiple ways to condition a single generative model. The model itself is shared; the inputs change.
For a generation of users who have never seen a command line,
are the new interface. A prompt is just text, but writing a good one is now its own small craft.
A few principles that hold across most chat and image tools:
about subject, style, context, and constraints.
— quote a sentence, paste an example, attach an image.
— generation is cheap. Treat the first output as a draft.
, not only the content — “in three sentences”, “as a bulleted list”, “as a poster in 1:2 ratio”.
— when the output is wrong, write down
it is wrong. That is often more informative than your initial prompt.
to prompts for language models. For now, the take-away is that the prompt
. (Even when it includes images, you usually still describe what to do in words.)
You will hear many architecture names in the wild. The four big families are:
— train two networks against each other: a
tries to produce realistic samples, a
tries to tell them apart from real data. Dominant for images 2014–2020, now mostly retired.
— predict the next token given the previous ones. Powers all chat models (next word) and many audio and image models. Underlying maths: factorise the joint distribution as a product of conditionals.
. Starting from random noise, the model iteratively removes a bit of noise to reveal a coherent image, audio, or video. Dominant for high-quality images, video, and (increasingly) audio.
— close cousin of diffusion models with simpler training. Powers some of the latest image and video models.
You do not need to memorise this. But when a tool brags about being “GAN-based” or “diffusion-based” or “flow-based”, you should be able to nod and understand roughly what that implies.
A short list, useful to keep in mind:
They do not know what is true.
A text model can produce a perfectly written paragraph that is factually wrong. An image model can produce a person with six fingers. We will return to this throughout the course.
They generalise from training data, not from causal understanding.
They have no model of physics, no model of social cause, no model of why things happen.
They are not deterministic at default settings.
They cannot remember you across sessions
unless they are explicitly designed to (with retrieval, memory, or fine-tuning).
They are bounded by their training cutoff.
Anything newer than the cutoff is invisible to them unless they can search the web or read uploaded files.
Pick one image tool you have access to.
Write a prompt that has a
“a watercolour illustration of a fox reading a book in a library, soft lighting, warm tones”
Generate the image with three different settings:
low CFG / guidance scale (or low temperature),
high CFG / guidance scale (or high temperature).
Paste the three results side by side and describe in two sentences each what changed.
Write down how strict the conditioning was — what is preserved, what changes?
Optional code track: sampling from a tiny language model

children.label: Optional code track: sampling from a tiny language model

children.value: library if it is not already installed and try:

from transformers import pipeline
gen = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt2")
for t in [0.2, 0.7, 1.2]:
    out = gen("Once upon a time in Oslo,", max_new_tokens=40, temperature=t, do_sample=True)
    print(f"--- t={t} ---")
    print(out[0]["generated_text"])
GPT-2 is small and quaint by 2026 standards but illustrates the temperature knob nicely.
Describe in plain language the difference between
. Why does that matter for creative work?
Tools like Midjourney expose only a few sampler parameters; tools like ComfyUI expose dozens. Whom does each design serve?
The same model, same prompt, two clicks: two different images. Is this a feature or a bug for your use case?
